summary: DHH argues that the wave of Windows worms and viruses (Blaster, Sobig) is the best possible marketing for Apple, since affected users only need one Mac-owning friend to learn that Macs don't suffer these problems. He acknowledges OS X isn't theoretically immune but notes its Unix core, smaller target profile, and Apple's better reputation all contribute to its safety. He expresses sympathy for those affected while noting he can't recall a single major OS X virus.
key_points: Real-world security disasters are more persuasive marketing than any ad campaign
OS X's Unix foundation and smaller market share make it practically safer than Windows
Word-of-mouth from Mac owners in mixed social circles is a powerful conversion tool
No operating system is truly immune, but practical risk matters more than theoretical vulnerability
money_quotes: No billion-dollar ad campaign could have positioned Apple as good as the latest swarm of worms and vira for Windows has.
This is average people getting hit big time without even opening Outlook.
There needs to be just one Mac owner in their circle of friends or acquaintances for the infected to get the message: This doesn't happen on a Mac.
It would be foolish to think that OS X is immune, no operating system is.
I've never even heard of a widespread OS X virus or worm.
key_insight: The best product marketing isn't advertising — it's your competitor's failures being felt firsthand by ordinary people.
